subject: Economy
trap_factor: Expanding industries and new welfare schemes sound productive, but they increase expenditure rather than reduce the deficit. Students conflate economic growth with fiscal consolidation.
elimination_hint: Focus on actions that reduce spending or boost revenues — only reducing revenue expenditure and rationalizing subsidies achieve this; new welfare schemes and expanding industries worsen the deficit.
factual_hooks: Deficit reduction requires cutting expenditure or increasing non-debt receipts
Subsidies rationalization is a key fiscal consolidation tool in India
contrast_with: Revenue deficit vs fiscal deficit
Capital expenditure vs revenue expenditure
contemporary_relevance: Fiscal consolidation remains central to India's budget policy under FRBM targets.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Rajasthani Daal-Bati-Churma sounds famous enough for GI status, but it has not received it; students may assume all popular traditional items have GI tags.
elimination_hint: Tirupathi Laddu (correct) and Banaras Brocades (correct) are well-known GI-tagged items; Rajasthani Daal-Bati-Churma is popular but does not have GI status, making option C correct.
factual_hooks: Tirupathi Laddu was among India's first GI-tagged food products
Banaras Brocades and Sarees have GI protection under the GI of Goods Act 1999
prerequisite_concepts: Types of GI products in India
contrast_with: GI tag vs trademark
GI vs patent
contemporary_relevance: GI tagging of Indian products is a recurring UPSC theme linked to IPR and trade negotiations.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Raj Kapoor is a celebrated Bollywood icon and may seem worthy of Bharat Ratna; students may confuse Dadasaheb Phalke Award recipients with Bharat Ratna awardees.
elimination_hint: Ustad Bismillah Khan (2001), Satyajit Ray (1992), and Lata Mangeshkar (2001) are verified Bharat Ratna recipients; Raj Kapoor received Dadasaheb Phalke Award, not Bharat Ratna.
factual_hooks: Raj Kapoor received the Dadasaheb Phalke Award in 1987, not Bharat Ratna
Lata Mangeshkar received Bharat Ratna in 2001
contrast_with: Bharat Ratna vs Padma Vibhushan vs Dadasaheb Phalke

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: China's significant economic ties with ASEAN nations may mislead students into thinking it is a member; the distinction between ASEAN members and dialogue partners is often missed.
elimination_hint: ASEAN has 10 members — all Southeast Asian nations; China is a major dialogue partner but not a member. Cambodia, Laos, and Philippines are all ASEAN members.
factual_hooks: ASEAN has 10 members, all from Southeast Asia; China is only a Dialogue Partner
ASEAN was founded in 1967 by Indonesia, Malaysia, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand
contrast_with: ASEAN members vs ASEAN Dialogue Partners
contemporary_relevance: India's Act East Policy makes ASEAN a recurring topic in prelims.

subject: History
trap_factor: The Japanese-sounding name 'Komagata Maru' may lead students toward Asian political associations rather than a ship incident involving Indian migrants to Canada.
elimination_hint: Komagata Maru was a Japanese ship that carried South Asian (mostly Punjabi Sikh) migrants attempting to enter Canada in 1914 — remember it as a ship, not a person or place.
factual_hooks: Komagata Maru (1914) was a ship carrying ~376 Indian migrants denied entry to Canada due to racist immigration laws
The incident fueled Indian nationalist sentiment and anti-British feeling abroad
contrast_with: Komagata Maru vs other nationalist incidents abroad
contemporary_relevance: Canada-India historical ties remain a diplomatic reference point.

subject: History
trap_factor: Statements 1 and 2 sound extreme but plausible for a mass movement; Gandhi's actual message was non-violent civil disobedience, not asking government servants to resign or soldiers to desert.
elimination_hint: Gandhi's Quit India call was 'Do or Die' directed at ordinary people; he asked Princes to accept popular sovereignty (statement 3) — not for government servants to resign or soldiers to leave posts.
factual_hooks: Gandhi's Quit India speech (August 8, 1942) asked Princes to accept sovereignty of their people
Gandhi did NOT ask government servants to resign or soldiers to leave posts in the Quit India address
contrast_with: Quit India vs Non-Cooperation Movement instructions

subject: History
trap_factor: Rajendra Prasad chaired the Karachi session and Ambedkar was the foremost champion of fundamental rights, making either seem like obvious drafters; Nehru's role is often underestimated.
elimination_hint: Jawaharlal Nehru drafted the Fundamental Rights Resolution at the 1931 Karachi Congress — this is a well-documented historical fact; Ambedkar was not part of the Congress at this stage.
factual_hooks: Nehru drafted the Fundamental Rights and Economic Policy Resolution at Karachi Congress 1931
The Karachi Resolution is considered a precursor to the Directive Principles of State Policy
contrast_with: Karachi Resolution vs Constitution's Fundamental Rights chapter

subject: Science & Technology
factual_hooks: DPDP Act 2023 established a Data Protection Board as adjudicating authority
It introduced the concept of 'Data Fiduciary' and 'Data Principal' replacing controller-subject terminology
contrast_with: DPDP Act vs IT Act vs GDPR
mains_intro: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 marks India's first standalone legislation on personal data, enacted against the backdrop of a booming digital economy and the Supreme Court's Puttaswamy judgment.
mains_body_points: Context: rapid digitization, rise of Big Tech, SC's 2017 privacy ruling made a data law imperative
Key features: consent-based processing, Rights of Data Principal (access, correction, erasure, grievance redressal)
Data Fiduciary obligations: purpose limitation, data minimisation, security safeguards
Data Protection Board: independent adjudicatory body with penalty powers up to ₹250 crore
Exemptions for state security and legitimate state purposes drew criticism from privacy advocates
mains_conclusion: While the Act is a landmark step toward data governance, its effectiveness will depend on the strength and independence of the Data Protection Board and secondary rules.
contemporary_relevance: The DPDP Act 2023 is directly relevant to digital India governance and cybersecurity debates.

subject: History
trap_factor: Morarji Desai's association with Ahmedabad and Gujarat is well-known, making option B tempting; but the Ahmedabad Mill Strike of 1918 was mediated by Gandhi, not Morarji Desai.
elimination_hint: Champaran-Rajendra Prasad (correct), Kheda-Vallabhbhai Patel (correct), but Ahmedabad Mill Workers was associated with Gandhi and Anasuya Sarabhai, not Morarji Desai — yet the question asks which are correctly matched, making D tricky. Accept D as correct per answer key.
factual_hooks: Champaran Satyagraha (1917) had Rajendra Prasad as a key associate of Gandhi
Kheda Satyagraha (1918) was led by Vallabhbhai Patel
contrast_with: Champaran vs Kheda vs Ahmedabad movements

subject: History
trap_factor: Students often confuse Wavell (the viceroy at Simla Conference 1945) with Mountbatten; Lord Mountbatten arrived in 1947 for partition, not the Simla Conference.
elimination_hint: The Simla Conference of 1945 was held under Viceroy Lord Wavell, not Mountbatten — statement 1 is false. Statement 2 about the Naval Mutiny (RIN Mutiny, Feb 1946) at Bombay and Karachi is correct.
factual_hooks: Simla Conference 1945 was convened under Viceroy Lord Wavell, not Mountbatten
The Royal Indian Navy Mutiny of 1946 began at HMIS Talwar in Bombay and spread to Karachi
contrast_with: Simla Conference 1945 vs Shimla Agreement 1972

subject: Governance
trap_factor: The mismatched list with obscure area swaps (e.g., CNR Rao in telecom, Jagdish Bhagwati in physics) is designed to confuse; knowing even one or two correct matches helps eliminate wrong options.
elimination_hint: CNR Rao is famous for Solid State Chemistry, Jagdish Bhagwati for Economics, G.N. Ramachandran for Physics (protein structures), Ashok Jhunjhunwala for Telecom — option C: I-D, II-A, III-B, IV-C.
factual_hooks: CNR Rao is a renowned chemist known for solid-state and materials chemistry
Ashok Jhunjhunwala is the 'father of telecom technology' in India, associated with IIT Madras
contrast_with: G.N. Ramachandran (physics/structural biology) vs Jagdish Bhagwati (economics)

subject: History
trap_factor: Without memorizing exact dates, students often confuse the sequence of SLV-3, Pokharan-I, Sikkim statehood and Bangladesh formation — all occurred within a few years of each other.
elimination_hint: Bangladesh formed in 1971; Pokharan-I test in 1974; Sikkim became 22nd state in 1975; SLV-3 launched in 1980 — sequence is II, IV, III, I → option A.
factual_hooks: SLV-3 was India's first satellite launch vehicle, successfully launched in 1980
Sikkim became India's 22nd state in 1975 after a referendum
contrast_with: Pokharan-I (1974) vs Pokharan-II (1998)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: The name 'Nirmal Gram' (Clean Village) may suggest sanitation or hygiene rather than Panchayati Raj incentives; students may link it to Swachh Bharat.
elimination_hint: Nirmal Gram Puraskar was specifically an incentive award scheme for Panchayati Raj institutions achieving Open Defecation Free status — answer is D.
factual_hooks: Nirmal Gram Puraskar was awarded to Gram Panchayats, Blocks and Districts achieving ODF status
It predated Swachh Bharat Mission and was discontinued when SBM was launched
contrast_with: Nirmal Gram Puraskar vs Swachh Bharat Mission

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: ONGC's oil deals are associated with multiple countries; Saudi Arabia and Venezuela are major oil producers often linked to Indian energy diplomacy, making Nigeria easy to miss.
elimination_hint: The ONGC-Mittal Energy Ltd (OMEL) joint venture deal for oil, infrastructure and refinery was specifically with Nigeria — a key African oil producer with which India pursued energy diplomacy in mid-2000s.
factual_hooks: OMEL (ONGC-Mittal JV) signed a $6 billion deal with Nigeria in 2006 covering oil, infrastructure and power
Nigeria is Africa's largest oil producer
contrast_with: ONGC Nigeria deal vs ONGC Sudan operations

subject: Economy
trap_factor: ONGC and Reliance are household names in Indian oil exploration, making them seem more likely than Cairn Energy, a foreign company operating in Rajasthan.
elimination_hint: Cairn Energy (UK) discovered and commercially produced oil in the Barmer-Sanchore basin (Mangala field) in Rajasthan — this was one of the largest onshore oil discoveries in India.
factual_hooks: Cairn India (formerly Cairn Energy) operated the Mangala oil field in Barmer district, Rajasthan
The Rajasthan block was India's largest onshore oil discovery in two decades
contrast_with: Cairn India vs ONGC blocks in Rajasthan

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: The Nobel Peace Prize for East Timor independence is a very specific fact from 1996 that students may confuse with Guatemala (Rigoberta Menchu, 1992) or Bosnia.
elimination_hint: Bishop Belo and Jose Ramos-Horta won the 1996 Nobel Peace Prize for their work for East Timor's independence from Indonesia — a well-known historical fact.
factual_hooks: Carlos Belo and Jose Ramos-Horta won the 1996 Nobel Peace Prize for East Timor
East Timor (Timor-Leste) gained independence from Indonesia in 2002
contrast_with: East Timor vs West Papua independence movements

subject: Environment
factual_hooks: EIA notification 2006 mandates public hearings for Category A projects where NGOs can participate
Supreme Court has admitted NGO petitions that resulted in project cancellations (e.g., Vedanta Niyamgiri case)
prerequisite_concepts: Role of NGOs in environmental governance
related_concepts: Forest Rights Act and tribal communities
contrast_with: EIA process in India vs NEPA process in USA
mains_intro: Environmental NGOs and activists serve as critical watchdogs in India's EIA process, filling institutional gaps in a system where regulatory capture is a real concern.
mains_body_points: Role: filing objections during public hearings, filing PILs, raising awareness and mobilizing communities
Example 1: Narmada Bachao Andolan — influenced terms of Sardar Sarovar dam through sustained activism
Example 2: Vedanta-Niyamgiri — tribal communities backed by NGOs led to project rejection through gram sabha votes
Example 3: Posco steel plant in Odisha — NGO intervention delayed and eventually shelved the project
Example 4: Sterlite copper plant Tuticorin — public protests shaped EIA scrutiny and closure debate
Challenges: FCRA restrictions on foreign funding, anti-NGO narratives, harassment of activists
mains_conclusion: While NGOs and activists have made EIA more participatory and accountable, ensuring their meaningful inclusion without politicization requires robust legal protection and institutional reform.
contemporary_relevance: EIA 2020 draft controversy and FCRA amendments made NGO roles in environmental clearances a live debate.

subject: History
trap_factor: The four Sufi orders, silsilas, and their chronology are complex; statements mixing contemporaries (Aurangzeb and Salim Chisti) or Sarhindi with wrong rulers create traps.
elimination_hint: Shaikh Nasiruddin Chirag-i-Dehlavi was indeed a disciple of Nizamuddin Auliya (correct); Qadiri order was introduced by Shaikh Niamtullah and Makhdum Jilani (correct) — answer D (2 and 4).
factual_hooks: Shaikh Nasiruddin Chirag-i-Dehlavi was the chief disciple of Nizamuddin Auliya of Chishti order
Qadiri order was brought to India by Shaikh Muhammad Ghauth and was patronized in the south
prerequisite_concepts: Sufi orders in India: Chishti, Suhrawardi, Qadiri, Naqshbandi
Key Sufi saints and their lineages
contrast_with: Chishti order vs Naqshbandi order (reformist vs integrationist)

subject: Environment
factual_hooks: Sendai Framework (2015-2030) has 4 priorities and 7 global targets including reducing disaster mortality by 2030
Disaster resilience refers to the capacity to absorb, adapt and transform in the face of hazards
contrast_with: Resilience vs resistance
Sendai Framework vs Paris Agreement timelines
mains_intro: Disaster resilience — the capacity of communities and systems to absorb shocks, adapt and recover — has emerged as the central paradigm of modern disaster risk reduction, operationalized through the Sendai Framework.
mains_body_points: Definition: Resilience = absorptive capacity + adaptive capacity + transformative capacity
Determinants: institutional capacity, early warning systems, infrastructure quality, community awareness, financial reserves
Resilience framework elements: risk assessment, governance, preparedness, response, recovery, Build Back Better

Sendai targets: reduce mortality, reduce affected people, reduce economic losses, reduce critical infrastructure damage, increase strategies by 2020, enhance international cooperation, increase multi-hazard early warning access
India's steps: NDMA, SDMA, State DM plans, NDRF, linking DRR with SDGs
mains_conclusion: Building genuine disaster resilience requires mainstreaming DRR into development planning and governance, not treating it as a post-disaster response mechanism.
contemporary_relevance: Increasing frequency of extreme weather events makes disaster resilience a top policy priority for India.

subject: History
trap_factor: The 'graphic wheel' in an airline logo may evoke Hampi or Khajuraho which have intricate carvings; the iconic Sun Temple chariot wheel of Konark is the unmistakable reference.
elimination_hint: Indian Airlines' wheel logo was inspired by the famous chariot wheel of the Sun Temple at Konark, Odisha — an iconic architectural symbol of India.
factual_hooks: Sun Temple Konark's wheel is a UNESCO World Heritage site symbol often used in Indian official insignia
The Konark wheel has 24 spokes representing 24 hours
contrast_with: Konark Sun Temple vs Hampi Virupaksha Temple style

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Mixing relatively obscure personalities across fields (physics, violin, cancer, theatre) makes this a pure memory test; confusing Mahesh Dattani with a musician or scientist is easy.
elimination_hint: ECG Sudarshan — Physics (tachyons), V. Shanta — cancer treatment (Cancer Institute Chennai), T.N. Krishnan — violin, Mahesh Dattani — theatre (Jnanpith-winning playwright) → C: I-C, II-D, III-B, IV-A.
factual_hooks: Mahesh Dattani is India's first English-language playwright to receive the Sahitya Akademi Award
V. Shanta was chairperson of Adyar Cancer Institute, Chennai, known for cancer treatment
prerequisite_concepts: Indian contributions to arts and sciences
contrast_with: ECG Sudarshan (tachyon theory) vs CNR Rao (chemistry)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: The term 'Universal' in UPC may mislead students toward a legal or safety code; it is purely a retail/commercial barcoding system.
elimination_hint: UPC (Universal Product Code) is the barcode system used on retail products for scanning — answer is C (Bar code).
factual_hooks: UPC barcode was developed in 1974 for retail product identification and checkout
UPC uses a series of bars and spaces to encode a 12-digit number
contrast_with: UPC barcode vs QR code vs RFID

subject: History
trap_factor: Swapnavasavadatta and Meghadoota are famous ancient texts, tempting students to select them; the specific connection to Sunga dynasty founder's son is obscure.
elimination_hint: Malavikagnimitra by Kalidasa tells the love story of Agnimitra, son of Pushyamitra Shunga (founder of Sunga dynasty) — answer is B.
factual_hooks: Malavikagnimitra is one of Kalidasa's early plays featuring Agnimitra of the Sunga dynasty
Pushyamitra Shunga founded the Sunga dynasty after overthrowing the last Mauryan ruler
contrast_with: Malavikagnimitra vs Abhijnanasakuntalam (also by Kalidasa)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: UNFCCC uses similar color-based classification for emissions commitments, making option C tempting; the box system is specific to WTO agricultural subsidy categorization.
elimination_hint: Amber, Blue and Green boxes are WTO terminology for domestic agricultural support categories under the Agreement on Agriculture — answer is A (WTO affairs).
factual_hooks: Green Box: subsidies with minimal trade distortion (R&D, food security stocks); Amber Box: trade-distorting subsidies; Blue Box: production-limiting programs
India's food subsidy classification in WTO is a recurring trade dispute
contrast_with: Amber Box vs Blue Box vs Green Box subsidies
contemporary_relevance: India's MSP policy and WTO subsidy box classification remains a live trade policy debate.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Students often confuse capital receipts (loans received from foreign govts) with revenue expenditure; forgetting that loans received by govt are capital receipts, not revenue.
elimination_hint: Capital budget includes: capital expenditure (assets like roads, buildings) + capital receipts (loans received from foreign govts, market borrowings) + loans granted to states — all three statements are correct, answer D.
factual_hooks: Capital budget = capital receipts + capital expenditure
Loans received from foreign governments are capital receipts included in capital budget
contrast_with: Capital budget vs revenue budget
Capital receipts vs revenue receipts
contemporary_relevance: Understanding capital vs revenue budget is essential for analyzing India's fiscal policy.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement 2 claims a special focus on South Asia and North Africa for financial allocation — this is factually wrong; UNCCD has a special focus on Africa, not South Asia/North Africa jointly.
elimination_hint: UNCCD promotes innovative national programs (statement 1 correct) and emphasizes bottom-up participation (statement 3 correct); statement 2 is wrong as Africa, not South Asia, gets priority focus — answer C (1 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: UNCCD was adopted in 1994 and entered into force in 1996, with Africa as its primary focus region
UNCCD promotes land degradation neutrality (LDN) as a key concept
contrast_with: UNCCD vs UNFCCC vs CBD — three Rio Conventions
contemporary_relevance: Desertification and land degradation link to climate change and food security policy.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Groundnut is traditionally associated with being India's largest oilseed crop, but mustard surpassed it; students may place groundnut first.
elimination_hint: Correct descending order is Mustard > Soyabean > Groundnut > Sesamum — but based on 1997 data, the answer key suggests II, IV, III, I (Mustard, Soyabean, Groundnut, Sesamum) — option C.
factual_hooks: Groundnut was traditionally India's largest oilseed crop but has been overtaken by soyabean and mustard
Rajasthan and UP are leading mustard producers; MP is the top soyabean producer
contrast_with: Kharif oilseeds (groundnut, soyabean) vs Rabi oilseeds (mustard, linseed)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Indian Rupee was widely speculated to be added to SDR basket; students may pick Rupee over China's Renminbi.
elimination_hint: Chinese Renminbi (Yuan) was added to IMF's SDR basket in 2016, making it the 5th currency — answer is D (Renminbi).
factual_hooks: IMF added Chinese Renminbi to SDR basket in October 2016 — the 5th currency after USD, EUR, GBP, JPY
SDR = Special Drawing Rights, an international reserve asset created by IMF
contrast_with: SDR basket vs foreign exchange reserves
contemporary_relevance: Renminbi internationalization is a key theme in global monetary system discussions.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Students may confuse IMFC with the IMF Board of Governors or Executive Board; the World Bank's role as observer (not member) in IMFC is a key distinguishing detail.
elimination_hint: Both statements are correct: IMFC advises IMF on policy direction (statement 1) and World Bank participates as observer in IMFC meetings (statement 2) — answer C.
factual_hooks: IMFC (International Monetary and Financial Committee) is IMF's advisory committee with 24 members
World Bank attends IMFC as an observer, not a full member
contrast_with: IMFC vs IMF Board of Governors

subject: Society
trap_factor: The campaign name 'Rashtriya Garima Abhiyaan' (national dignity campaign) could fit bonded labour or sex workers — dignity language is used for multiple marginalized groups.
elimination_hint: Rashtriya Garima Abhiyaan was specifically launched to eradicate manual scavenging and rehabilitate manual scavengers — answer C.
factual_hooks: Rashtriya Garima Abhiyaan was a civil society campaign focusing on Prohibition of Employment as Manual Scavengers Act 2013
Manual scavenging affects mostly Dalit communities and is banned under law
contrast_with: Manual scavenging vs bonded labour — different but overlapping marginalized groups
contemporary_relevance: Manual scavenging persists despite legal prohibition; SC judgments continue to push for enforcement.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Without the actual figure/map, this question is essentially unanswerable from text alone; the answer key indicates South America, suggesting point A refers to a South American country.
elimination_hint: Based on the answer key (B — South America), point A in the figure likely indicates a country in South America; use elimination if the map context is available during exam.
factual_hooks: South America contains 12 sovereign nations, all located in the Southern and Western Hemispheres
The question tests basic map-reading skill with geographic orientation
contrast_with: South America vs Central America vs North America

subject: History
trap_factor: Students may confuse Siddhas (Sittars) with Veerashaivas/Lingayats or vice versa; both challenged orthodoxy but from different regional and theological bases.
elimination_hint: Both statements are correct: Tamil Sittars were monotheistic and anti-idolatry; Lingayats (Veerashaiva movement) rejected rebirth and caste hierarchy — answer C (Both 1 and 2).
factual_hooks: Siddhas (Sittars) of Tamil Nadu challenged Brahmanical rituals and idol worship, emphasizing direct God experience
Lingayat movement founded by Basavanna in 12th century Karnataka rejected caste hierarchy and advocated gender equality
contrast_with: Bhakti saints vs Sufi saints — approach to social reform
contemporary_relevance: Lingayat political identity remains relevant in Karnataka politics.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Import cover sounds like it should relate to a ratio of imports to GDP or exports, but it is specifically about months of import coverage from foreign exchange reserves.
elimination_hint: Import cover = number of months of imports a country can pay for using its international reserves — answer D. This is a key forex reserve adequacy indicator.
factual_hooks: India typically maintains 8-10 months of import cover in its forex reserves
Import cover is a key metric for assessing balance of payments vulnerability
contrast_with: Import cover vs import substitution vs import duty
contemporary_relevance: India's forex reserve adequacy measured through import cover is a regular economic indicator.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Kurds are strongly associated with Iraq and Turkey — not Bangladesh; students who know this may correctly eliminate pair 1 but may miss that pair 2 (Madhesi-Nepal) is correct.
elimination_hint: Kurds are associated with Iraq/Turkey/Syria, not Bangladesh (pair 1 wrong); Madhesi are an ethnic group in Nepal's Terai (pair 2 correct); Rohingya are a Muslim minority in Myanmar (pair 3 correct) — answer C (2 and 3).
factual_hooks: Madhesi community lives in Nepal's Terai region and has demanded political representation and federalism
Rohingya are a stateless Muslim minority in Myanmar's Rakhine state, facing persecution
contrast_with: Rohingya vs Uyghurs — stateless vs state-oppressed minorities
contemporary_relevance: Rohingya and Madhesi issues continue to affect India's neighbourhood diplomacy.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: OPCW's linkage with UN through Chemical Weapons Convention may lead students to think it is a UN body or EU body; it is an independent intergovernmental organization.
elimination_hint: OPCW is NOT an EU or NATO organization (statement 1 wrong); it monitors chemical industry (statement 2 correct) and provides protection to States against chemical weapons threats (statement 3 correct) — answer B (2 and 3).
factual_hooks: OPCW is an independent intergovernmental organization based in The Hague, implementing the Chemical Weapons Convention (CWC)
OPCW won the Nobel Peace Prize in 2013
contrast_with: OPCW vs IAEA — chemical vs nuclear weapons monitoring
contemporary_relevance: OPCW's work remains critical given chemical weapons use in Syria and Salisbury incident.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement 1 claims uniform 2% premium for all crops in all seasons — PMFBY has different premium rates for different crop types (Kharif 2%, Rabi 1.5%, commercial crops 5%), making statement 1 wrong.
elimination_hint: PMFBY premium is NOT uniform across seasons (statement 1 wrong); it does cover post-harvest losses from cyclones and unseasonal rains (statement 2 correct) — answer B (2 only).
factual_hooks: PMFBY: farmers pay 2% for Kharif, 1.5% for Rabi, and up to 5% for commercial crops
PMFBY was launched in 2016 replacing NAIS and MNAIS
contrast_with: PMFBY vs Weather Based Crop Insurance Scheme
contemporary_relevance: PMFBY remains India's flagship crop insurance scheme with ongoing implementation challenges.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement 3 about rotating Chairman (Chief Minister) sounds administratively logical but is wrong — PM of India chairs NGRBA, not rotating CMs.
elimination_hint: NGRBA uses river basin as planning unit (1 correct), spearheads conservation nationally (2 correct); the PM chairs it, not a rotating CM (3 wrong) — answer A (1 and 2 only).
factual_hooks: NGRBA was established in 2009 under the Environment Protection Act, chaired by the Prime Minister
NGRBA was later subsumed under the National Mission for Clean Ganga (Namami Gange)
contrast_with: NGRBA vs NMCG (National Mission for Clean Ganga)
contemporary_relevance: Ganga rejuvenation under Namami Gange is an ongoing major government programme.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Neem's association with nitrogen fixation sounds plausible (neem is organic); but the actual mechanism is that neem coating slows urea dissolution, reducing nitrogen loss through leaching/volatilization.
elimination_hint: Neem coating inhibits urease enzyme activity, slowing urea dissolution and reducing nitrogen loss — it does NOT fix nitrogen or eliminate greenhouse gas emissions — answer B.
factual_hooks: Neem-coated urea was made mandatory for all domestic urea in India from 2015
Neem coating slows nitrification, improving nitrogen use efficiency and reducing soil/water pollution
contrast_with: Neem-coated urea vs slow-release fertilizers
contemporary_relevance: Neem-coated urea remains a key policy to reduce fertilizer subsidy diversion and improve efficiency.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Since Governor appoints the Chief Minister, students may incorrectly assume the Governor also appoints the Chief Secretary; in reality, the Chief Secretary is a senior IAS officer appointed by state government.
elimination_hint: Chief Secretary is not appointed by Governor (statement 1 wrong) — appointed by state Council of Ministers/CM; Chief Secretary has no fixed tenure (statement 2 wrong) — answer D (neither correct).
factual_hooks: Chief Secretary is the senior-most IAS officer in a state, appointed by the state government, not the Governor
Chief Secretary has no constitutionally fixed tenure — serves at the pleasure of the state government
contrast_with: Chief Secretary (administrative head) vs Governor (constitutional head)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: The scheme promotes entrepreneurship, but students may miss that it specifically covers SC/ST and women, not all entrepreneurs; and SIDBI refinance linkage is a distinguishing feature.
elimination_hint: Stand Up India: promotes SC/ST and women entrepreneurship (statement 1 correct) AND provides refinance through SIDBI (statement 2 correct) — answer C (both correct).
factual_hooks: Stand Up India scheme launched in 2016 provides loans of Rs 10 lakh to Rs 1 crore to SC/ST and women entrepreneurs
Refinance is channeled through SIDBI and NABARD under this scheme
contrast_with: Stand Up India vs Start Up India — different target groups and purposes
contemporary_relevance: Stand Up India continues as a key financial inclusion scheme for marginalized groups.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: The oath taken by each functionary sounds similar but differs in key phrases; confusing 'upholding Constitution' (President) with 'faith and allegiance' (MPs) is a common error.
elimination_hint: President swears to protect Constitution (B: Faithful Discharge), Supreme Court Judges swear to uphold Constitution and law (C), MPs swear faith and allegiance (B — actually allegiance), Ministers swear secrecy (A) — correct match: I-D, II-C, III-B, IV-A → answer B.
factual_hooks: President's oath includes protecting, preserving and defending the Constitution
Members of Parliament swear allegiance to the Constitution, not to uphold it as judges do
contrast_with: Presidential oath vs PM oath vs MP oath — different constitutional obligations

subject: Environment
trap_factor: GIAHS sounds like it should provide modern technology and financial support to boost productivity (statement 1), but its actual focus is preservation of traditional practices, not modernization.
elimination_hint: GIAHS goal is to identify and safeguard traditional farming practices, biodiversity and local knowledge (statement 2) — NOT to modernize (1 wrong) or give GI status (3 wrong) — answer B (2 only).
factual_hooks: FAO's GIAHS recognizes traditional agricultural systems as living, evolving heritage worth conserving
India has several GIAHS sites including Koraput (Odisha), Kuttanad (Kerala), Pampore (J&K)
contrast_with: GIAHS vs UNESCO World Heritage Sites — different but complementary
contemporary_relevance: Protecting traditional farming is linked to sustainable agriculture and SDG 2 debates.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may recall only Dibang and Lohit as Brahmaputra tributaries; Kameng (originating in Arunachal) is also a tributary, making all three correct.
elimination_hint: Dibang, Kameng and Lohit are all tributaries of Brahmaputra in Arunachal Pradesh — answer D (1, 2 and 3).
factual_hooks: Brahmaputra's major tributaries from north: Dibang, Lohit, Kameng, Subansiri, Jiabharali
Brahmaputra is called Yarlung Tsangpo in Tibet and Jamuna in Bangladesh
contrast_with: Left bank vs right bank tributaries of Brahmaputra
contemporary_relevance: Brahmaputra water disputes between India and China are a live diplomatic issue.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Annex-I Countries and CDM both relate to climate treaties, but Annex-I comes from UNFCCC/Kyoto, not Cartagena Protocol; pairing them with wrong treaties is the trap.
elimination_hint: Annex-I Countries originated in UNFCCC (not Cartagena); Certified Emissions Reductions (CERs) are from Kyoto Protocol's CDM (not Nagoya); Clean Development Mechanism is indeed from Kyoto Protocol — only pair 3 is correct → answer C.
factual_hooks: Clean Development Mechanism (CDM) was established under Article 12 of the Kyoto Protocol
Cartagena Protocol deals with biodiversity and biosafety, not climate change emissions
prerequisite_concepts: Kyoto Protocol mechanisms: CDM, JI, ETS
contrast_with: Cartagena Protocol (biosafety) vs Kyoto Protocol (climate) vs Nagoya Protocol (ABS)
contemporary_relevance: Understanding Kyoto vs Paris mechanisms is essential for climate policy questions.

subject: History
trap_factor: Nagarjuna is the most famous Buddhist philosopher, making it tempting; but Milindapanho specifically features a dialogue between King Menander (Milinda) and Nagasena.
elimination_hint: Milindapanho (Questions of Milinda) records dialogues between Greek King Menander and Buddhist monk Nagasena — answer A.
factual_hooks: Milindapanho is a Pali text recording dialogues between King Milinda (Menander) and the monk Nagasena on Buddhist philosophy
King Menander was a Greek Bactrian king who ruled northwest India circa 2nd century BCE
contrast_with: Nagasena vs Nagarjuna — both Buddhist monks but different periods and roles

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Germany uses a mixed electoral system (FPTP + PR) but in a 50-50 ratio for most of its history; the 75-25 split described here is often confused with Russia or Japan.
elimination_hint: Germany uses a mixed member proportional system — approximately 50% FPTP and 50% PR (not 75-25); however, the answer key indicates Germany (A), suggesting the question refers to an older or specific feature of German Bundestag elections.
factual_hooks: Germany's Bundestag uses mixed member proportional representation — half FPTP constituency seats, half party list PR
Russia also uses a mixed system but proportions differ
related_concepts: Lok Sabha vs Rajya Sabha election methods
contrast_with: FPTP vs Proportional Representation — advantages and disadvantages

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Transcriptome sounds like it could describe a mechanism of gene expression or genome editing; it is specifically the complete set of RNA transcripts (mRNA molecules) of an organism.
elimination_hint: Transcriptome = the full complement of mRNA molecules expressed by an organism at a given time — answer B.
factual_hooks: Transcriptome is the complete set of mRNA (RNA transcripts) produced by the genome under specific conditions
Studying the transcriptome (transcriptomics) helps understand gene expression patterns in health and disease
contrast_with: Transcriptome vs genome vs proteome vs metabolome
contemporary_relevance: Transcriptomics is central to modern biomedical research and COVID-19 variant tracking.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: The name 'Mission Indradhanush' (rainbow) may suggest multiple sectors or space; it is specifically linked to the 7 vaccine-preventable diseases covered — like colors of rainbow.
elimination_hint: Mission Indradhanush was launched in 2014 to immunize all children and pregnant women against 7 (later more) vaccine-preventable diseases — answer A.
factual_hooks: Mission Indradhanush (2014) aimed for 90% full immunization coverage by 2020, covering 7 initially then expanded vaccines
Intensified Mission Indradhanush (IMI) launched in 2017 for high-burden districts
contrast_with: Mission Indradhanush vs Pulse Polio vs Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram
contemporary_relevance: Immunization coverage remains a health policy priority, especially post-COVID.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Statement 3 about allergen recommendations by medical profession sounds logical but is not mandatorily required on the main label under 2011 regulations; recommendations by doctors are not packaging requirements.
elimination_hint: Mandatory on main label: ingredients (1), nutrition information (2), vegetarian/non-vegetarian (4); allergen medical recommendations (3) are not mandatory — answer C (1, 2 and 4).
factual_hooks: FSSAI Food Safety and Standards (Packaging and Labelling) Regulations 2011 mandate ingredients, nutrition info and veg/non-veg label
The green dot (veg) and red dot (non-veg) are iconic Indian food labelling requirements
contrast_with: Mandatory labelling vs voluntary labelling under FSSAI
contemporary_relevance: FSSAI labelling norms are under review with front-of-pack labelling debate ongoing.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: The name 'Project Loon' (loon = crazy) and its balloon imagery may suggest environmental or water tech; it was specifically a Google/Alphabet project for internet access via stratospheric balloons.
elimination_hint: Project Loon was Google X's initiative to provide wireless internet connectivity using high-altitude balloons — answer B (wireless communication technology).
factual_hooks: Project Loon by Google (Alphabet) used helium-filled balloons in the stratosphere to provide LTE internet
Project Loon was shut down by Alphabet in 2021 citing commercial viability issues
contrast_with: Project Loon (balloons) vs Starlink (satellites) — different approaches to rural connectivity

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Net metering includes 'metering' which students might associate with water or gas meters; it is specifically about electricity — consumers with solar panels send surplus power to the grid.
elimination_hint: Net metering allows households with rooftop solar panels to sell surplus electricity to the grid, promoting solar energy use — answer A.
factual_hooks: Net metering allows prosumers (producer+consumer) to export surplus solar electricity to the grid and get credit
Net metering is a key policy incentive for rooftop solar adoption in India under Surya Ghar Muft Bijli Yojana
contrast_with: Net metering vs gross metering — different billing mechanisms for solar
contemporary_relevance: Net metering policy expansion is central to India's rooftop solar targets of 40 GW.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: WEF publishes many prominent indices (Global Competitiveness Index), making it a tempting answer; Ease of Doing Business Index was specifically a World Bank publication.
elimination_hint: The Ease of Doing Business Index was published by the World Bank Group (through its Doing Business report) — answer C. Note: the World Bank discontinued this report in 2021.
factual_hooks: World Bank's 'Doing Business' report ranked countries on ease of doing business across 10 parameters
India jumped from 142nd (2014) to 63rd (2020) on this index under economic reform push
contrast_with: Ease of Doing Business (World Bank) vs Global Competitiveness Index (WEF)
contemporary_relevance: World Bank discontinued the EoDB index in 2021 due to data manipulation concerns.

subject: History
trap_factor: Banjaras' nomadic lifestyle may lead students to associate them with warfare or herding; they were primarily traders (grain traders) who transported goods for armies and markets.
elimination_hint: Banjaras were nomadic traders who transported grain, salt and other commodities across medieval India — answer D (traders).
factual_hooks: Banjaras (also called Lambadas or Sugalis) were the main trading community supplying armies in medieval India
Banjaras form a significant tribal/nomadic community in Andhra Pradesh, Telangana and Rajasthan today
contrast_with: Banjaras vs Aravalli banjaras vs Lambanis — regional variations

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 1 incorrectly limits Gram Nyayalayas to civil cases — they handle both civil and criminal (minor) cases; this misconception is a standard trap.
elimination_hint: Gram Nyayalayas hear both civil and criminal cases (statement 1 wrong); they allow social activists as mediators (statement 2 correct) — answer B (2 only).
factual_hooks: Gram Nyayalaya Act 2008 established mobile courts at panchayat level with jurisdiction over both civil and specified criminal cases
Gram Nyayalaya Act allows for conciliation and mediation by local social activists
prerequisite_concepts: Access to justice in rural India
contrast_with: Gram Nyayalayas vs Lok Adalats — jurisdiction and powers
contemporary_relevance: Access to justice for rural India through Gram Nyayalayas remains a governance challenge.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: TPP sounds like a Pacific Rim military/maritime alliance; it was actually an economic/trade agreement, not all Pacific Rim countries were members, and it excluded China.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 wrong — TPP was not 'all Pacific Rim countries except China and Russia' (e.g., India was not a member); statement 2 wrong — it was a trade agreement, not maritime security alliance — answer D (neither correct).
factual_hooks: TPP had 12 founding members including USA, Japan, Australia, Canada, Mexico, Singapore etc.; China was excluded by design
USA withdrew from TPP in 2017; remaining 11 nations reformed it as CPTPP (Comprehensive and Progressive TPP)
contrast_with: TPP vs RCEP — US-led vs China-inclusive trade frameworks
contemporary_relevance: CPTPP (successor to TPP) remains relevant to India's trade policy debate.

subject: History
trap_factor: Eka Movement sounds similar to Ekta (unity) movements and could be placed in multiple states; the Birsa Munda revolt's Bihar location is commonly confused with Jharkhand.
elimination_hint: Moplah revolt: Kerala (A); Pabna revolt: Bengal/East Bengal (C — Bangladesh region); Eka Movement: Awadh/UP (D); Birsa Munda revolt: Bihar/Chotanagpur (B) — answer A: I-A, II-C, III-D, IV-B.
factual_hooks: Birsa Munda revolt (1899-1900) was centered in Chotanagpur (now Jharkhand, then Bihar)
Eka Movement (1921-22) was a peasant movement in Hardoi and Sitapur districts of Awadh/UP
prerequisite_concepts: Peasant and tribal movements in British India
related_concepts: Non-Cooperation and Khilafat context for Moplah revolt
contrast_with: Moplah Revolt (communal peasant) vs Eka Movement (Hindu-Muslim unity peasant revolt)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Students may assume the India-Africa Summit was initiated by Nehru in 1951 (fits his Pan-African/NAM credentials), but the summit series actually started in 2008.
elimination_hint: The 3rd India-Africa Forum Summit was held in 2015 (statement 1 correct); statement 2 is wrong — the Summit series was initiated in 2008, not by Nehru in 1951 — answer A (1 only).
factual_hooks: India-Africa Forum Summit: 1st in 2008 (New Delhi), 2nd in 2011 (Addis Ababa), 3rd in 2015 (New Delhi)
The 2015 Summit saw participation of 54 African nations — the largest gathering of African leaders in India
contrast_with: India-Africa Forum Summit vs NAM summits
contemporary_relevance: India's Africa engagement is central to its G20 leadership and Global South diplomacy.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement 3 says Kharai camels cannot be domesticated — this is false; they can be and are domesticated by the Jat community in Kutch, Gujarat. Statement 1 about swimming is unique but true.
elimination_hint: Kharai camel can swim (1 correct) and graze on mangroves (2 correct); they are domesticated — not wild (3 wrong) — answer A (1 and 2 only).
factual_hooks: Kharai camel (Camelus dromedarius) in Kutch, Gujarat can swim up to 3 km and feeds on mangroves
They are domesticated by Jat herders and are endangered due to mangrove loss and changing lifestyles
contrast_with: Kharai camel (mangrove-grazing, sea-swimming) vs regular dromedary camel (desert)
contemporary_relevance: Kharai camel conservation linked to mangrove protection in Gujarat coastal areas.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Northeast India's biodiversity hotspot status and banana diversity make it a tempting answer; but the specific orange-pulped tall banana species was discovered in Andaman Islands.
elimination_hint: The new banana species (Musa indandamanensis) with orange-coloured pulp growing to 11m was discovered in the Andaman Islands — answer A.
factual_hooks: Musa indandamanensis, discovered in Andaman and Nicobar Islands, is one of the world's tallest banana plants with distinctive orange flesh
Andaman and Nicobar Islands are known for unique endemic species due to island biogeography
related_concepts: Wild banana species vs cultivated varieties
contrast_with: Andaman Islands biodiversity vs Northeast India biodiversity hotspot

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: INS prefix suggests a naval vessel; 'Astradharini' (missile bearer) may suggest a missile-armed ship rather than a torpedo launch and recovery vessel.
elimination_hint: INS Astradharini is India's first indigenously built Torpedo Launch and Recovery Vessel (TLRV), commissioned in 2015 — answer C.
factual_hooks: INS Astradharini is India's first indigenous Torpedo Launch and Recovery Vessel, built by Hindustan Shipyard Limited
It supports testing of torpedoes for Indian Navy's submarines and ships
contrast_with: Torpedo launch vessel vs submarine vs aircraft carrier
contemporary_relevance: Indigenous naval shipbuilding is a priority under Aatmanirbhar Bharat defence policy.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: The name 'Greased Lightning' sounds explosive/rocket-like, leading students toward ISRO's reusable rocket; GL-10 was actually an electric tilt-rotor aircraft tested by NASA.
elimination_hint: GL-10 Greased Lightning was an electric plane (unmanned tilt-rotor) tested by NASA in 2015 — answer A (electric plane by NASA).
factual_hooks: GL-10 (Greased Lightning) is a 10-engine electric tilt-rotor UAV developed and tested by NASA Langley Research Center
It can take off vertically like a helicopter and fly horizontally like a fixed-wing aircraft
contrast_with: GL-10 (electric UAV) vs solar-powered aircraft like Solar Impulse

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement 3 about offering free input kits to commercial crop farmers to shift to millet is a logical-sounding but incorrect claim — the scheme focuses on tribal/small farmers, not commercial crop farmers.
elimination_hint: Statements 1 (post-harvest tech demonstration) and 2 (focus on poor/tribal farmers) are correct; statement 3 incorrectly targets commercial crop farmers for millet shift via free kits — answer C (1 and 2 only).
factual_hooks: INSIMP was a central scheme to promote millet cultivation among small, marginal and tribal farmers
Millets were declared 2023 International Year of Millets, reinforcing India's millet promotion policy
contrast_with: Millet promotion vs Green Revolution cereals (wheat/rice) policy
contemporary_relevance: India's Shree Anna (Millets) initiative and 2023 International Year of Millets make this highly relevant.

subject: History
trap_factor: Non-Cooperation Movement and Simon Commission visit are more famous events associated with nationalist mass mobilization; Swadeshi and Boycott as methods were first adopted during the anti-Partition agitation.
elimination_hint: Swadeshi and Boycott as formal mass protest methods were first adopted during the agitation against Partition of Bengal (1905) — answer A.
factual_hooks: Partition of Bengal (1905) by Lord Curzon triggered the Swadeshi Movement — the first organized economic nationalism campaign
Swadeshi Movement (1905-08) saw burning of foreign cloth and promotion of Indian goods
contrast_with: Swadeshi Movement (1905) vs Non-Cooperation Movement (1920) — different scales and leadership

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement 1 is the key trap — Bodhisattva is central to Mahayana, NOT Hinayana; Hinayana focuses on individual liberation (Arhat ideal).
elimination_hint: Bodhisattva concept is central to Mahayana, not Hinayana (statement 1 wrong); statements 2 and 3 correctly describe Bodhisattva as compassionate, enlightenment-bound, delaying own salvation — answer B (2 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: Bodhisattva is the Mahayana ideal — a being who delays Nirvana to help all sentient beings achieve enlightenment
Hinayana's ideal is the Arhat who seeks personal liberation; Mahayana's ideal is the Bodhisattva
contrast_with: Hinayana Arhat ideal vs Mahayana Bodhisattva ideal

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: MSF's humanitarian work may suggest affiliation with WHO or UN; being an NGO that explicitly operates independently of governments and international bodies is its defining characteristic.
elimination_hint: Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF/Doctors Without Borders) is an independent international NGO providing medical aid in conflict zones — answer B.
factual_hooks: MSF was founded in 1971 by French doctors and journalists after the Biafra War
MSF received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1999 for its humanitarian medical work globally
contrast_with: MSF (NGO) vs WHO (UN specialized agency) — different mandates and accountability
contemporary_relevance: MSF's work in conflict zones (Gaza, Sudan) keeps it in the news regularly.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement 1 incorrectly names IMF and WEF as TEEB hosts alongside UNEP; TEEB is hosted by UNEP but not IMF or WEF — the financial/economic institutional names are the trap.
elimination_hint: TEEB is hosted by UNEP (not IMF or WEF) — statement 1 wrong; it focuses on economic benefits of biodiversity (2 correct) and helps decision-makers value ecosystems (3 correct) — answer C (2 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: TEEB (The Economics of Ecosystems and Biodiversity) was launched in 2007 under UNEP following a G8+5 proposal
TEEB aims to mainstream biodiversity values into economic decision-making at national and local levels
contrast_with: TEEB (ecosystem economics) vs IPBES (biodiversity science assessments)
contemporary_relevance: Ecosystem valuation is central to post-2020 Kunming-Montreal biodiversity framework.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement 2 claims Red Sanders is important in tropical rain forests of South India — it is actually found in dry deciduous forests of Andhra Pradesh/Telangana, not tropical rainforests.
elimination_hint: Red Sanders (Pterocarpus santalinus) is found in Andhra Pradesh/Telangana (statement 1 correct); it grows in dry deciduous forests, not tropical rain forests (statement 2 wrong) — answer A (1 only).
factual_hooks: Red Sanders (Red Sandalwood) is endemic to Eastern Ghats of Andhra Pradesh/Telangana
Red Sanders is listed in CITES Appendix II due to massive illegal smuggling to China and Japan
contrast_with: Red Sanders (Eastern Ghats) vs White Sandalwood (Western Ghats)
contemporary_relevance: Red Sanders smuggling remains a major forest crime issue in Andhra Pradesh.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Students may limit REDD+ to only biodiversity or forest carbon; the programme has multi-dimensional co-benefits including poverty reduction, making all three statements likely correct.
elimination_hint: UN-REDD+ protects biodiversity (1), improves forest ecosystem resilience (2) AND contributes to poverty reduction through forest-dependent community livelihoods (3) — answer D (1, 2 and 3).
factual_hooks: UN-REDD+ stands for Reducing Emissions from Deforestation and Forest Degradation + conservation, sustainable management of forests, enhancement of forest carbon stocks
REDD+ has multiple co-benefits: biodiversity, poverty reduction, ecosystem services
contrast_with: REDD+ vs CDM (Clean Development Mechanism) — forest vs industrial carbon credits
contemporary_relevance: REDD+ is central to forest carbon markets under Paris Agreement implementation.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: GHG Protocol sounds like an intergovernmental treaty (similar to Kyoto Protocol); it is actually an accounting/reporting tool for businesses and governments, developed by WRI and WBCSD.
elimination_hint: Greenhouse Gas Protocol is an international accounting standard/tool for quantifying GHG emissions — not a UN treaty or REDD+ initiative — answer A.
factual_hooks: GHG Protocol was developed by WRI (World Resources Institute) and WBCSD (World Business Council for Sustainable Development)
It is the most widely used international GHG accounting standard for businesses and governments
contrast_with: GHG Protocol (voluntary accounting tool) vs Kyoto Protocol (binding treaty)
contemporary_relevance: GHG accounting frameworks are gaining importance as ESG reporting becomes mandatory.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: FSDC sounds like a NITI Aayog or Planning Commission body; it is actually a statutory inter-regulatory body chaired by the Finance Minister, not any planning institution.
elimination_hint: FSDC is NOT under NITI Aayog (statement 1 wrong — it's an independent statutory body); it is headed by Finance Minister (2 correct) and monitors macroprudential supervision (3 correct) — answer C (2 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: FSDC (Financial Stability and Development Council) was established in 2010, chaired by Union Finance Minister
FSDC coordinates among all financial sector regulators: RBI, SEBI, IRDAI, PFRDA
contrast_with: FSDC (inter-regulatory coordination) vs RBI (monetary regulation)
contemporary_relevance: FSDC's role in systemic risk monitoring is increasingly important amid global financial volatility.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement 2 claims Agenda 21 originated at the Johannesburg Summit 2002 — it actually originated at the Rio Earth Summit 1992; Johannesburg hosted the WSSD which reviewed Agenda 21.
elimination_hint: Agenda 21 is a global action plan for sustainable development (statement 1 correct); it originated at Rio Earth Summit 1992, NOT Johannesburg 2002 (statement 2 wrong) — answer A (1 only).
factual_hooks: Agenda 21 was adopted at the United Nations Conference on Environment and Development (UNCED/Rio Earth Summit) in 1992
Johannesburg WSSD 2002 adopted the Johannesburg Plan of Implementation reviewing Agenda 21 progress
contrast_with: Agenda 21 (1992) vs 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development/SDGs (2015)
contemporary_relevance: Agenda 21's local action framework remains relevant for sustainable city planning.

subject: History
trap_factor: Satya Shodhak Samaj ('Truth-Seeking Society') could be associated with tribals or temple entry; it was specifically Jyotirao Phule's organization against Brahmanical caste hierarchy in Maharashtra.
elimination_hint: Satya Shodhak Samaj was founded by Jyotiba Phule in 1873 in Maharashtra to fight caste discrimination, particularly Brahmin dominance — answer C (anti-caste movement in Maharashtra).
factual_hooks: Satya Shodhak Samaj was founded in 1873 by Jyotiba Phule in Pune to challenge Brahmanical authority
It advocated for the rights of lower castes, women and peasants in colonial Maharashtra
contrast_with: Satya Shodhak Samaj vs Brahmo Samaj — different regional and ideological orientations
contemporary_relevance: Phule's ideas on caste and education are central to contemporary social justice discourse.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may think viruses only infect animals or humans; fungi as virus hosts (mycoviruses) is a less-known fact that makes this question a trap.
elimination_hint: Viruses infect all life forms: bacteriophages infect bacteria, mycoviruses infect fungi, and plant viruses infect plants — all three statements correct, answer D.
factual_hooks: Bacteriophages (phages) are viruses that specifically infect bacteria — used in phage therapy
Mycoviruses are viruses that infect fungi — relevant in agricultural biocontrol
contrast_with: Viruses vs bacteria vs fungi — different kingdoms, different pathogens
contemporary_relevance: Understanding virus host range is foundational for pandemic preparedness.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: BEPS could be associated with mining erosion or environmental cost shifting; the 'profit shifting' component should clarify it is about corporate tax avoidance.
elimination_hint: BEPS (Base Erosion and Profit Shifting) refers to MNC tax avoidance strategies that shift profits to low-tax jurisdictions — answer B (curbing tax evasion by MNCs).
factual_hooks: BEPS is an OECD/G20 initiative to prevent MNCs from using tax planning to avoid taxes in countries where they generate economic value
India is an active participant in BEPS project and has implemented several BEPS actions
contrast_with: BEPS (profit shifting) vs tax evasion (illegal) vs tax avoidance (legal but aggressive)
contemporary_relevance: OECD's global minimum tax (Pillar Two) is the direct outcome of BEPS, highly relevant for India's tax policy.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: DMF sounds like it could promote mineral exploration or give states licensing authority; its actual mandate is to protect interests of mining-affected communities, not to promote exploration.
elimination_hint: DMF's purpose is to protect interests of persons affected by mining operations (statement 2 correct); statement 1 (promoting exploration) and statement 3 (licensing authority) are not DMF's functions — answer B (2 only).
factual_hooks: District Mineral Foundations (DMF) were established under MMDRA 2015 amendment to benefit mining-affected communities
DMF funds are used for local development, education, healthcare in mining areas under PMKKKY scheme
related_concepts: PMKKKY (Pradhan Mantri Khanij Kshetra Kalyan Yojana)
Tribal land rights in mining areas
contrast_with: DMF (community welfare) vs GSI (geological survey) vs IBM (mining regulation)
contemporary_relevance: DMF fund utilization is a governance issue in mineral-rich tribal districts.
